text: We present an unusual case of bladder mass and its laparoscopic management. A fifty-one-year-old female presented with history of irritative L U T S for last one year with no history of hematuria, tobacco use, and comorbidities. The physical examination was normal. CT abdomen showed a heterogeneous mass in right lateral and anterior wall with calcification and hypodense area in the mass measuring four into three centimeters in size without perivesical extension and regional lymphadenopathy with normal adnexa bilaterally.

During cystoscopy, a large grayish-white mass with hair follicles and soft yellowish necrotic tissue is visible arising from the dome of the bladder. The rest of the urinary bladder and ureteric orifices appear normal.

The histopathology showed keratinized stratified squamous epithelium, presence of hair follicles, adipocytes, mucous glands in the subepithelium, with no evidence of malignancy. Therefore, final diagnosis of benign primary urinary bladder dermoid cyst was made.

The surgeon performs a laparoscopic transperitoneal partial cystectomy in reverse Trendelenburg position. Standard ports are configured, and the anterior bladder wall is separated from the abdominal wall using a harmonic scalpel with traction and counter traction. The right ovary and fallopian tube, adhered to the anterior bladder wall near the dermoid cyst, are carefully dissected using a harmonic scalpel and an endogeovascular stapler. The bladder is further detached from lateral attachments, revealing the dermoid cystic mass at the dome region. Tumor excision begins with a one-centimeter margin of normal bladder mucosa, using a harmonic scalpel, and hairs are visible protruding from the mass. The tumor is completely excised.

The surgeon closes the bladder wall using freehand intracorporeal suturing with 3-0 V-Loc sutures, starting from the distal aspect and moving towards the proximal portion of the anterior bladder wall. The bladder is then distended with 200 ml of normal saline to verify the watertight closure, showing no leakage upon inspection.

The surgeon retrieves the excised tumor specimen using an endo-catch bag. The specimen appears well-defined, grayish-brown with a tuft of hair and normal surrounding bladder tissue. The cut surface of the tumor shows a yellowish appearance. A drain is placed post-retrieval.

So, we can conclude that if a mass with peripheral calcification with hair growth is found on anterior wall or dome of bladder, a dermoid cyst should be considered as a differential diagnosis. Primary dermoid cyst of urinary bladder can be managed by laparoscopic partial cystectomy.
